grammarLessons.name: 有 & 没有 — Have / Don't Have
了 — Completed Action & Change of State
Question Words — 什么, 谁, 哪里...

cards.grammarNote:
**你好** (nǐ hǎo) = hello, lit. 'you good.' It works any time of day.
**你** (nǐ) = you (2nd tone); **好** (hǎo) = good (3rd tone). Two 3rd tones in a row: the first rises to 2nd tone in natural speech — so it sounds like 'níhǎo.'
**吗** (ma) at the end of a statement turns it into a yes/no question: 你好吗？= 'Are you well?'

**请** (qǐng) precedes a verb to make a polite request: 请坐 (please sit), 请问 (may I ask).
For offering something: 请 alone. To ask for an item: 给我... 好吗? or just 来一个... (bring one...).
cards.phraseRomanized: Qǐng gěi wǒ yī bēi kāfēi.

cards.grammarNote:
**谢谢** (xièxie) = thank you — the character **谢** means 'to thank.' It's doubled for warmth; doubling a verb or adjective in Chinese is a common softening pattern.
**非常感谢** (fēicháng gǎnxiè) = thank you very much (more formal). **感谢** (gǎnxiè) = to feel grateful — a more literary word.
Response: **不客气** (bù kèqi) or **没事** (méi shì).

**想要** (xiǎng yào) = would like (softer, more polite)
**要** (yào) = want/will (more direct, also used for future)
**想** alone = think/feel like doing
In a restaurant, 我要... is common and perfectly natural.
cards.phraseRomanized: Wǒ xiǎng yào yī bēi kāfēi.

cards.grammarNote:
**需要** (xūyào) = need (a genuine necessity): 我需要帮助 (I need help).
**要** (yào) = need/want (can overlap with need in casual speech): 我要水 (I need/want water).
**必须** (bìxū) = must / have to (stronger obligation): 你必须来 (You must come).

**我叫** (wǒ jiào) = my name is (lit. 'I am called') — more natural in conversation than 我的名字是.
**我姓**... (wǒ xìng...) = my surname is... — used when giving a last name in formal contexts.

**我** (wǒ) = I / me (same form for subject and object — no case distinction).
**我的** (wǒ de) = my. The particle **的** (de) marks possession.
Note: in fast speech, 我的 before a close relationship (friend, family) often drops the 的: 我朋友 = my friend, 我妈 = my mom.

**你** (nǐ) = you (everyday)
**您** (nín) = you (formal/respectful) — used with elders, customers, or in formal settings
cards.phraseRomanized: Nǐ zhōngwén shuō de hěn hǎo.

cards.grammarNote:
**他** (tā) = he/him. In speech, **他** (he), **她** (she), and **它** (it) are all pronounced tā — they are identical in spoken Chinese, distinguished only in writing.
**他们** (tāmen) = they (male or mixed group); **她们** (tāmen) = they (female group only). Both sound the same when spoken.
**他** (he) and **她** (she) are both pronounced tā — identical in speech, different only in writing. Context makes the meaning clear in conversation.

**晚安** (wǎn'ān) = good night — **晚** (wǎn) means evening/late, **安** (ān) means peace/safe. Lit. 'peaceful evening.'
The apostrophe in wǎn'ān separates two syllables to avoid ambiguity (wǎnān could be misread as wǎ-nān).
**好好休息** (hǎohǎo xiūxi) = rest well. Doubling an adjective (好好) turns it into an adverb: 'properly, nicely.'

**吗** (ma) is the yes/no question particle — add it to the end of any statement to turn it into a question: 你好 → 你好吗？
Answer pattern: **我很好** (wǒ hěn hǎo) = I'm good. The **很** (hěn) is required in adjective sentences even when not strongly emphasizing 'very' — dropping it sounds unnatural unless making a contrast.
More colloquial: **你怎么样？** (nǐ zěnmeyàng?) = How's it going?
cards.englishPhrase: How are you? I'm fine, thanks.
cards.phraseRomanized: Nǐ hǎo ma? Wǒ hěn hǎo, xièxie.

cards.grammarNote:
**高兴** (gāoxìng) = happy/pleased — **高** (gāo) = tall/high, **兴** (xīng/xìng) = excitement/interest. Together: 'high spirits.'
**认识** (rènshi) = to know/meet (a person). Distinguished from **知道** (zhīdào) = to know a fact.
**很高兴认识你** is the standard first-meeting phrase. After getting to know someone: **很高兴见到你** (nice to see you again).

**不客气** (bù kèqi) = you're welcome, lit. 'don't be polite/formal.'
**客气** (kèqi) = polite, formal, standing on ceremony. Telling someone 不客气 is a warm way to say 'no need to thank me.'
Alternatives: **没事** (méi shì) = no problem/it's nothing; **不用谢** (bú yòng xiè) = no need to thank me.

**它** (tā) = it (for non-human things and animals). Pronounced the same as **他** (he) and **她** (she) — all are tā.
In practice, Chinese often drops the pronoun entirely when context is clear: 那是什么？(What is that?) — no 'it' needed.
**这** (zhè) = this; **那** (nà) = that — often used instead of 'it' to refer to specific things.

**我们** (wǒmen) = we/us — **我** (I) + **们** (men, pluralizing suffix).
The suffix **们** (men) makes pronouns plural: 你们 (you all), 他们 (they).
Note: 咱们 (zánmen) = we including the listener; 我们 can exclude the listener. This distinction is more common in northern dialects.

**他们** (tāmen) = they (male or mixed group). **她们** (tāmen) = they (all female). Both sound identical in speech.
**一起** (yīqǐ) = together — always placed before the verb: 他们一起吃饭 (they eat together), not *他们吃饭一起.
**吃饭** (chīfàn) = to eat (a meal). Lit. 'eat rice' — **饭** (fàn) = cooked rice/meal, the staple that represents eating.

**这** (zhè) = this; **这个** (zhège) = this one (with the measure word 个).
In Chinese, a measure word (**量词**) goes between number/demonstrative and noun: 这本书 (this book — 本 is the measure word for books).

**那个** (nàge) = that one — **那** (nà) = that + **个** (gè) the general-purpose measure word.
Tone note: **那** (nà) = that (4th tone) vs **哪** (nǎ) = which (3rd tone) — easily confused in speech.
**那个人是我的朋友** — **的** (de) connects adjectives/possessives to nouns: 我的朋友 = my friend.
cards.phraseRomanized: Nàge rén shì wǒ de péngyou.

cards.grammarNote:
**我的** (wǒ de) = my — the particle **的** (de) marks possession, like 's in English.
Structure: possessor + 的 + noun: 我的名字 (my name), 他的书 (his book).
With close relationships, **的** is often dropped in casual speech: 我妈 (my mom), 我朋友 (my friend).
**名字** (míngzi) = name — **名** (míng) = fame/name, **字** (zì) = character/word.

**谁** (shéi, also pronounced shuí) = who. Chinese question words stay in the position where the answer would go — they do not move to the front: 那个人是谁？(That person is who?) mirrors the answer 那个人是我朋友 (That person is my friend).
**谁的** (shéi de) = whose: 这是谁的书？(Whose book is this?)

**什么** (shénme) = what — stays in the position of the answer: 你想要什么？(You want what?) mirrors 你想要咖啡 (You want coffee).
**什么** can also mean 'any' in negative sentences: 我什么都不知道 (I don't know anything — lit. 'whatever, I don't know').
**什么时候** (shénme shíhou) = when (lit. 'what time').

**什么时候** (shénme shíhou) = when, lit. 'what time/moment.' **时候** (shíhou) = time/moment.
In Chinese, time expressions go before the main verb: 你什么时候来？(When are you coming? lit. 'You what-time come?')
For clock times use **几点** (jǐ diǎn) = what o'clock: 你几点来？(What time are you coming?)

**哪里** (nǎlǐ) = where — **哪** (nǎ, 3rd tone) = which + **里** (lǐ) = inside/place.
Tone alert: **那里** (nàlǐ, 4th tone) = there vs **哪里** (nǎlǐ, 3rd tone) = where. One tone makes a big difference!
**在** (zài) before 哪里 = 'at where' — for location questions the structure is: topic + 在 + 哪里: 洗手间在哪里？
**洗手间** (xǐshǒujiān) = bathroom, lit. 'wash-hand-room.'

**为什么** (wèishénme) = why, lit. 'for what.' **为** (wèi) = for/because of + **什么** (shénme) = what.
**因为** (yīnwèi) = because (to answer why): 因为我有事 (because I'm busy / have something going on).
**为什么...不能来** — **不能** (bù néng) = cannot. **不** + **能** = not + able. Compare: 不想来 (don't want to come) vs 不能来 (can't come).

**怎么** (zěnme) = how (in what way): 怎么去？(How do you get there?)
**怎么样** (zěnmeyàng) = how is it? / what's it like? — asking for an assessment or status.
**怎么** before a verb asks about the method; **怎么样** at the end asks for an opinion or evaluation.
**怎么** can also express surprise: 你怎么知道？(How do you know?!)

**早上好** (zǎoshang hǎo) = formal good morning; **早** (zǎo) = casual good morning
**下午好** (xiàwǔ hǎo) = good afternoon; **晚上好** (wǎnshang hǎo) = good evening
你好 works any time of day.
cards.englishPhrase: Good morning! Did you sleep well?
cards.phraseRomanized: Zǎoshang hǎo! Shuì de hǎo ma?

cards.grammarNote:
**找** (zhǎo) = to look for / to search for. Compare: **看** (kàn) = to look at (not searching).
**在找** = currently looking for — **在** before a verb marks an ongoing action (like English '-ing').
**出口** (chūkǒu) = exit, lit. 'go-out mouth.' **入口** (rùkǒu) = entrance, lit. 'enter-mouth.' Both built on **口** (kǒu) = mouth/opening.

**不好意思** (bù hǎo yìsi) = excuse me / I'm embarrassed to ask — the most natural all-purpose attention-getter
**打扰一下** (dǎrǎo yīxià) = sorry to interrupt
**请问** (qǐng wèn) = may I ask — great before a question
cards.englishPhrase: Excuse me, where is the subway?
cards.phrase: 不好意思，请问地铁站在哪里？
cards.phraseRomanized: Bù hǎo yìsi, qǐngwèn dìtiě zhàn zài nǎlǐ?

cards.grammarNote:
**能** (néng) = can (physical ability or circumstance): 我能游泳 (I can swim)
**可以** (kěyǐ) = may/can (permission or possibility): 我可以进来吗? (May I come in?)
**会** (huì) = can (learned skill): 我会说中文 (I can speak Chinese)

**知道** (zhīdào) = to know (a fact, information). **知** (zhī) = to know/perceive + **道** (dào) = road/way — 'to know the way of something.'
**认识** (rènshi) = to know (a person or be familiar with).
Negation: **不知道** (bù zhīdào) — note that **不** changes from 4th tone to 2nd tone before another 4th-tone syllable: bú zhīdào.
cards.englishPhrase: I don't know where it is.

cards.grammarNote:
**有** (yǒu) = have or there is/are: 我有钱 (I have money), 这里有咖啡 (There is coffee here).
Negated: **没有** (méiyǒu) — never 不有, always 没有. 没有 alone = I don't have it / there isn't any.
cards.englishPhrase: I'm hungry. Do you have water?
cards.phraseRomanized: Wǒ è le. Nǐ yǒu shuǐ ma?

cards.grammarNote:
**做** (zuò) = to do / to make (a thing or activity): 做饭 (cook), 做作业 (do homework).
**在做** = currently doing — **在** before a verb indicates an ongoing action.
**干** (gàn) = to do (colloquial, slightly informal): 你在干什么？(What are you up to?)
**搞** (gǎo) = to do / get (colloquial): 你在搞什么？(What are you doing? — can sound impatient.)

**吃** (chī) = to eat. **吃饭** (chīfàn) = to eat a meal (lit. 'eat rice/food') — a culturally important phrase; 你吃饭了吗？(Have you eaten?) is a common greeting.
**在哪里吃饭** — location phrases (在 + place) come before the verb in Chinese: 在哪里吃 = 'at where eat,' not *吃在哪里.

**喜欢** (xǐhuān) = to like. **喜** (xǐ) = joy/happiness + **欢** (huān) = joyful/welcome. Together they mean 'joyfully welcome.'
**喜欢** takes a noun or verb directly: 我喜欢咖啡 (I like coffee), 我喜欢喝咖啡 (I like drinking coffee).
**爱** (ài) = to love (stronger): 我爱你 (I love you). Using 爱 for objects is also natural: 我爱咖啡 (I love coffee).

**但是** (dànshì) = but (formal/written). **但** (dàn) alone is more common in speech.
**可是** (kěshì) = but (casual, common in daily speech).
**虽然...但是** (suīrán...dànshì) = although...but — unlike English, Chinese uses both 'although' AND 'but': 虽然贵，但是很好 (although it's expensive, it's very good).
cards.englishPhrase: I want to go, but I can't.
cards.phraseRomanized: Wǒ xiǎng qù, dànshì bù néng.

cards.grammarNote:
**和** (hé) = and, connecting nouns: 水和食物 (water and food).
**还有** (háiyǒu) = also/and also, adding items: 还有一件事 (and there's one more thing).
和 does not connect verbs or sentences — use **然后** (ránhòu) for 'and then'.

**还是** (háishì) = or (in questions): 你要茶还是咖啡？(Tea or coffee?)
**或者** (huòzhě) = or (in statements): 你可以喝茶或者咖啡 (You can have tea or coffee)
A common mistake is swapping them.

**了** (le) after an adjective or verb marks a change of state: 饿了 = have become hungry, 渴了 = have become thirsty.
我很饿 = I am hungry (general state)
我饿了 = I've gotten hungry (new state)

**渴** (kě) = thirsty. The character contains the water radical **氵** (sān diǎn shuǐ, three-drop water) on the left — many water-related characters share this component.
**渴了** = have become thirsty (change of state, with **了**).
**渴望** (kěwàng) = to crave/yearn — the same 渴 used figuratively for intense desire.

**人** (rén) = person/people. One of the simplest Chinese characters — it looks like a person walking (two strokes like two legs).
**人** is used to form nationalities: **美国人** (měiguó rén) = American (lit. 'beautiful-country person'), **中国人** (zhōngguó rén) = Chinese person.
Also used in compounds: **朋友** = friend, but **好人** = a good person, **大人** (dàrén) = adult.

**地方** (dìfāng) = place (general). **地** (dì) = ground/earth + **方** (fāng) = direction/area.
**地点** (dìdiǎn) = specific location/venue (more formal, used for events).
**哪里** (nǎlǐ) = where — also used as a humble response to compliments: 哪里哪里！ = 'oh not at all!' (lit. 'where where!').
cards.englishPhrase: Do you know a good place?
cards.phraseRomanized: Nǐ zhīdào yīgè hǎo dìfāng ma?

cards.grammarNote:
**东西** (dōngxi) = a physical thing or stuff: 买东西 (buy things / go shopping)
**事情** (shìqing) = a matter or situation: 有事情 (have something going on / there's a matter)
Don't mix them up.

**这里** (zhèlǐ) = here — **这** (zhè, this) + **里** (lǐ, inside/place). Also: **这儿** (zhèr) = here (northern dialect, more casual).
**那里** (nàlǐ) = there; **哪里** (nǎlǐ) = where.
**过来** (guòlái) = come over (toward speaker). Compare: **过去** (guòqù) = go over (away from speaker).

**那里** (nàlǐ) = there — **那** (nà, 4th tone) + **里** (lǐ). Also: **那儿** (nàr, northern dialect).
Tone alert: **那里** (nàlǐ, 4th tone) = there vs **哪里** (nǎlǐ, 3rd tone) = where.
Structure: 洗手间在那里 — the location (那里) follows **在** (zài) at the end: subject + 在 + location.

**很** (hěn) = very, but in adjective sentences it's often required even without emphasis: 我很好 (I'm good) — dropping 很 makes it sound like a contrast.
**非常** (fēicháng) = extremely; **太** (tài) = too/so: 太好了！(So great!)

**再见** (zàijiàn) = goodbye, lit. 'again see.' **再** (zài) = again + **见** (jiàn) = to see/meet.
This is why it mirrors English 'see you again.'
**明天见** (míngtiān jiàn) = see you tomorrow — the pattern [time] + 见 works for any time: 下周见 (see you next week), 一会儿见 (see you in a bit).
**拜拜** (bāibāi) = bye-bye (casual, borrowed from English).

**水** (shuǐ) = water. The character is a pictograph of flowing water.
**一杯水** (yī bēi shuǐ) = a glass/cup of water — **杯** (bēi) is the measure word for cups and glasses.
**热水** (rèshuǐ) = hot water; **冷水** (lěngshuǐ) = cold water; **开水** (kāishuǐ) = boiled water (very common in China — tap water is typically boiled before drinking).
cards.phraseRomanized: Qǐng gěi wǒ yī bēi shuǐ.

cards.grammarNote:
**食物** (shíwù) = food (general/formal). **食** (shí) = to eat/food + **物** (wù) = thing/matter.
**吃的** (chī de) = food (colloquial, lit. 'things to eat') — more natural in everyday speech.
**饭** (fàn) = cooked rice/meal — the most common everyday word for food: 吃饭 (eat a meal), 做饭 (cook).
**好吃** (hǎochī) = delicious, lit. 'good to eat' — a very common compliment for food.

**家** (jiā) = home (warm, where you belong): 回家 (go home), 在家 (at home).
**房子** (fángzi) = house (the physical building).
家 also means 'family': 我家有四口人 (My family has four people).

**商店** (shāngdiàn) = store/shop. **商** (shāng) = commerce/trade + **店** (diàn) = shop/establishment.
**店** appears in many business names: 咖啡店 (coffee shop), 书店 (bookstore), 餐厅 (restaurant — a slightly fancier word).
**关门** (guānmén) = to close (a door/business), lit. 'close door.' **开门** (kāimén) = to open. **了** marks the new state: 商店关门了 = the store has closed.

**现金** (xiànjīn) = cash. **现** (xiàn) = present/current + **金** (jīn) = gold/metal/money.
**刷卡** (shuākǎ) = swipe a card (pay by card), lit. 'swipe card.' **刷** (shuā) = to brush/swipe.
In modern China, **微信支付** (Wēixìn zhīfù, WeChat Pay) and **支付宝** (Zhīfùbǎo, Alipay) are far more common than cash or cards.

**帮助** (bāngzhù) = help (noun or formal verb). **帮** (bāng) alone = to help (casual verb): 帮我 (help me), 帮个忙 (do me a favor).
**忙** (máng) = busy — contrast 帮忙 (bāngmáng) = to help out (a set phrase, lit. 'help with busyness').
**救命！** (jiùmìng!) = Help! (emergency, lit. 'save life!').

**洗手间** (xǐshǒujiān) = bathroom/restroom, lit. 'wash-hand-room' — the polite word used in public.
**厕所** (cèsuǒ) = toilet/restroom (more direct, also common).
**卫生间** (wèishēngjiān) = bathroom (lit. 'hygiene room') — used in hotels and formal contexts.
Character breakdown: **洗** (xǐ) = wash, **手** (shǒu) = hand, **间** (jiān) = room/space.

**总是** (zǒngshì) = always (habitual). **总** (zǒng) = all/total/always.
Frequency adverbs go before the verb in Chinese: 我早上总是喝咖啡 (I always drink coffee in the morning).
**经常** (jīngcháng) = often; **有时候** (yǒu shíhou) = sometimes; **偶尔** (ǒu'ěr) = occasionally — all follow the same pre-verb placement rule.
cards.englishPhrase: I always drink coffee in the morning.

cards.grammarNote:
**从不** (cóngbù) = never (habitual)
**从来不** (cónglái bù) = have never
**从来没有** (cónglái méiyǒu) = have never done: 我从来没有去过日本 (I've never been to Japan)
All three place the negative before the verb.

**去** (qù) = to go (away from the speaker). **来** (lái) = to come (toward the speaker). These are direction-dependent: use 去 when moving away, 来 when moving toward.
**去** + place: 去商店 (go to the store). **去** + verb: 去吃饭 (go eat).
**回去** (huíqù) = go back; **出去** (chūqù) = go out.

**来** (lái) = to come (toward the speaker or a reference point).
**从...来** (cóng...lái) = come from: 你从哪里来？(Where are you from?)
**来** also functions as a filler before an action to indicate 'let's do this': 我来 (I'll do it / let me), 来一杯 (bring a cup / let's have a cup).

**明白** (míngbai) = to understand (mentally grasp). **明** (míng) = bright/clear + **白** (bái) = white/clear. Together: 'completely clear.'
**懂** (dǒng) = to understand (comprehend): 我不懂 (I don't get it). More conversational.
**听懂** (tīng dǒng) = to understand by listening; **看懂** (kàn dǒng) = to understand by reading.

**说** (shuō) = to speak/say.
**会说** (huì shuō) = can speak (learned skill): 你会说中文吗？
**讲** (jiǎng) = to speak/tell/explain (more formal, also used for lectures): 讲中文, 讲故事 (tell a story).
**聊** (liáo) = to chat casually: 我们聊聊吧 (let's have a chat).

**给** (gěi) = to give; also marks the recipient of an action (like 'to' or 'for').
请给我菜单 = 'please give me the menu.' The structure: 给 + recipient + object.
**给** as benefactive: 我给你做饭 (I'll cook for you) — 给你 = 'for you.'
Dual-object: 给 takes both a recipient and an object, like 'give someone something.'

**看** (kàn) = to look at / watch / read: 看书 (read a book), 看电视 (watch TV)
**见** (jiàn) = to see / meet: 见面 (meet up)
我看不见 = I can't see it (visibility blocked); 看不到 = can't see it (too far/hidden)

**和** (hé) = and/with (connecting nouns and people). 我和朋友 = me and my friend / with my friend.
**一起** (yīqǐ) = together — often paired with 和: 和...一起 = together with...
**的** (de) at the end of this sentence: 我和朋友一起来的 — the sentence-final 的 indicates the sentence explains a past fact or the manner/circumstance of how something happened.
cards.phraseRomanized: Wǒ hé péngyou yīqǐ lái de.

cards.grammarNote:
**没有** (méiyǒu) = without / don't have / there is no.
For 'without' in a request, Chinese uses 不加 (bù jiā) = 'don't add': 咖啡不加糖 = coffee without sugar (lit. 'coffee don't-add sugar').
**加** (jiā) = to add. Common in food orders: 不加冰 (no ice), 不加糖 (no sugar), 多加奶 (extra milk).

**为了** (wèile) = for the purpose of / in order to (indicating purpose or motivation).
**给** (gěi) = for (beneficiary): 这是给你的 (this is for you — for the benefit of you).
**这是给你的** — the sentence-final **的** here turns the phrase into a 'it is the case that' emphasis: 'this is indeed for you.'
cards.englishPhrase: I run every day for my health.

cards.grammarNote:
**在** (zài) = at/in/on — covers location: 在家 (at home), 在桌子上 (on the table).
在…上 (shàng) = on top of; 在…里 (lǐ) = inside; 在…下 (xià) = under
The position word follows the place noun.
cards.englishPhrase: The book is on the table.

cards.grammarNote:
**从** (cóng) = from (a starting point in space or time): 从哪里来 (come from where), 从早到晚 (from morning to night).
**从** vs **离** (lí): 从 = 'starting from'; 离 = 'away from, distance from': 我家离这里不远 (my home isn't far from here).
**来自** (láizì) = come from / originate from (slightly more formal than 从...来).

**好** (hǎo) = good/well (3rd tone). One of the most versatile characters in Chinese.
好 as a response = OK / sure: 好，我去 (OK, I'll go).
**好 + verb** = easy to verb: 好吃 (good to eat = delicious), 好喝 (good to drink = tasty), 好用 (easy to use).
**好主意** (hǎo zhǔyi) = good idea — **主意** (zhǔyi) = idea/plan.

**不好** (bù hǎo) = not good. For 'bad' as in terrible: **糟糕** (zāogāo) = awful/terrible.
**太糟糕了** = 'that's terrible!' — **太...了** is an intensifier (too/so): 太好了！(Great!), 太贵了！(Too expensive!)
**了** at the end of 太...了 reinforces the exclamation and marks it as a changed or present state.

**大** (dà) = big/large (4th tone). One of the simplest and most productive characters.
**大杯** (dàbēi) = large cup/size; **中杯** (zhōngbēi) = medium; **小杯** (xiǎobēi) = small.
**来一杯** = 'bring a cup' — **来** used as a filler for ordering: 来一个汉堡 (one hamburger, please). Very natural in ordering contexts.

**小** (xiǎo) = small (3rd tone). Often used affectionately as a prefix for names: **小李** (Xiǎo Lǐ) = 'little Li' (friendly nickname for someone younger or close).
**一张** (yī zhāng) — **张** (zhāng) is the measure word for flat objects: tables, paper, tickets, faces.
Contrast with **杯** (bēi) for cups, **本** (běn) for books, **件** (jiàn) for clothing.
cards.phraseRomanized: Qǐng gěi wǒ yī zhāng xiǎo zhuōzi.

cards.grammarNote:
**很多** (hěn duō) = a lot, many, much — **多** (duō) = many/much. Unlike standalone adjectives, **多** used as a pre-noun modifier doesn't need a measure word: 很多工作 (a lot of work).
**多少** (duōshǎo) = how much/how many (question word).
**多** also appears in commands: 多喝水！(drink more water!), 多吃点！(eat more!).
cards.englishPhrase: I have a lot of work.

cards.grammarNote:
**一点** (yīdiǎn) = a little, a bit — used before a noun (一点水, a little water) or after an adjective to soften it (好一点, a bit better).
**有点** (yǒudiǎn) = somewhat, a little — used before adjectives/verbs to indicate a mild degree, often with a slightly negative flavor: 有点贵 (a bit expensive), 有点累 (a little tired).
Difference: 一点 modifies nouns; 有点 modifies adjectives/verbs.

**用中文怎么说** = 'how do you say [it] in Chinese?' — **用** (yòng) = using, by means of. 用中文 = in Chinese (lit. 'using Chinese').
**怎么** (zěnme) before a verb = how to (do the verb): 怎么说 = how to say, 怎么写 = how to write, 怎么去 = how to get there.
This pattern is extremely useful for language learners!
cards.englishPhrase: How do you say 'thank you' in Chinese?

cards.grammarNote:
**帮** (bāng) = to help (verb). Here it takes a double object: 帮 + 我 (me) + 拿 (carry) — 'help me carry.'
**拿** (ná) = to hold/carry/take with hands. Compare: **带** (dài) = to bring/take along, **搬** (bān) = to move something heavy.
**吗** (ma) turns any statement into a yes/no question. Without 吗: 你能帮我拿这个 = 'you can help me carry this' (statement).
cards.englishPhrase: Can you help me carry this?
cards.phraseRomanized: Nǐ néng bāng wǒ ná zhège ma?

cards.grammarNote:
**多少钱** (duōshǎo qián) = how much money? — the standard way to ask a price. Placed at the end: topic + 多少钱？
**件** (jiàn) is the measure word for upper-body garments (jackets, shirts, coats) and also used with luggage/cases.
**钱** (qián) = money. **块** (kuài) = the colloquial word for yuan (RMB): 三块钱 = 3 yuan.
cards.englishPhrase: How much does this jacket cost?

cards.grammarNote:
**对不起** (duìbuqǐ) = I'm sorry (genuine apology — lit. 'I can't face you')
**不好意思** (bù hǎo yìsi) = excuse me / I'm embarrassed — lighter, more social
**抱歉** (bàoqiàn) = formal apology

**没问题** (méi wèntí) = no problem, lit. 'no question/issue.' **问题** (wèntí) = question or problem.
**别担心** (bié dānxīn) = don't worry. **别** (bié) = don't (imperative negative, more casual than 不要).
**担心** (dānxīn) = to worry, lit. 'carry-heart' — **担** (dān) = to carry on a shoulder pole + **心** (xīn) = heart.
